metadata.career_relevance: Razorpay's payment service has 800+ unit tests passing on H2 in-memory DB — then fails in production on PostgreSQL because H2 doesn't enforce foreign key constraints by default, and its JSONB support is different. Testcontainers: run the REAL PostgreSQL, Redis, and Kafka in Docker during tests. Bugs caught in tests, not production. After this topic, you can write integration tests that are identical to production behavior.

hook.real_world_problem:
Razorpay payment service tests:

```
H2 in-memory tests (passing):
  @DataJpaTest ✓ (all 400 tests green)
  Deploy to production...
  PROD ERROR: column 'data' is of type jsonb but expression is of type varchar
  H2 accepts VARCHAR for JSONB column; PostgreSQL rejects it
  3-hour outage. Manual data fix. Postmortem.

Testcontainers (catches it in CI):
  @Testcontainers
  @Container static PostgreSQLContainer<?> postgres = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:15");
  
  CI: starts real PostgreSQL in Docker container
  Test: INSERT with VARCHAR into JSONB → PSQLException
  Build: FAILED (bug caught in CI, not production!)
  Fix: change @Column(columnDefinition='TEXT') to 'JSONB'
  Build: passes. Deploy: no issue.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Integration tests using PostgreSQL, Redis, and Kafka containers that mirror production exactly.

visual_aid.description: Two parallel flows. Left (H2 tests): Java test → H2 in-memory DB (fake). Bug in production because H2 ≠ PostgreSQL. Right (Testcontainers): Java test → Docker daemon → PostgreSQL container (real) → real JDBC connection. Same PostgreSQL version as production. CI/CD pipeline shows Docker socket mounted in GitHub Actions. Container lifecycle: @BeforeAll start, @AfterAll stop. Container reuse: static @Container = one container for all test methods.
visual_aid.alt_text: Testcontainers: real Docker containers in tests vs H2 fake database

worked_example.scenario: Razorpay payment repository test with real PostgreSQL and Kafka.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. PostgreSQLContainer + @DynamicPropertySource
2. PaymentRepository test with JSONB column
3. KafkaContainer: produce + consume payment event
4. Container reuse with static @Container

step_by_step_solution.details: H2 problems: (1) JSONB → H2 accepts any string (no validation); (2) PostgreSQL-specific SQL (RETURNING, ON CONFLICT, ARRAY operators) fails on H2; (3) H2 sequence behavior differs; (4) H2 full-text search doesn't exist. Testcontainers starts the REAL database in Docker — same version as production. Same SQL, same constraints, same behavior. CI requirement: Docker daemon running (standard in GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins).

worked_example.expected_output:
[Testcontainers] Starting postgres:15-alpine container...
[Testcontainers] Container started: postgres://rzp:test@localhost:54321/payments_test
[Test] saveAndFind_withRealPostgres PASSED
[Test] jsonbColumn_enforcedByPostgres PASSED
[Testcontainers] Stopping container...
things_to_notice: Static @Container: one container shared across all test methods in the class. Container starts once in @BeforeAll, stops in @AfterAll. Non-static = new container per method (much slower).
@DynamicPropertySource: overrides Spring Boot's datasource properties at test startup with the container's dynamic port (Testcontainers maps to a random host port). Must be static method.
Container reuse (advanced): `.withReuse(true)` keeps containers alive across test runs (CI/CD optimization). Requires `testcontainers.reuse.enable=true` in ~/.testcontainers.properties.
Docker prerequisite: Testcontainers requires Docker daemon running. Works out-of-box on local dev, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI. Configure Docker socket path for custom CI.

industry_examples.use_case: Payment service CI: 40 PostgreSQL integration tests with Testcontainers. Container starts once per CI job (shared across test classes via container reuse). Test run time: 45s (containers) vs 2s (H2) — worth it for catching 23 production-like bugs in the last quarter. Kafka container: 15 event-driven tests verify exactly what consumers receive, not mocks.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you test database interactions in Spring Boot?' → Testcontainers for integration tests. @DataJpaTest + @Testcontainers + @Container PostgreSQLContainer runs the real PostgreSQL version. @DynamicPropertySource injects the dynamic JDBC URL. Benefits: catches DB-specific bugs (JSONB, constraints, sequences), real Kafka for event tests, mirrors production exactly. Trade-off: slower than H2 (30-60s startup) but worth it for integration test accuracy.
applications.real_world_workflow: Testcontainers workflow: (1) add testcontainers dependencies, (2) replace H2 with @Container PostgreSQLContainer, (3) @DynamicPropertySource overrides datasource properties, (4) run tests: Docker pulls image on first run, cached on subsequent runs, (5) CI: ensure Docker daemon available, (6) container reuse: `.withReuse(true)` for faster iteration.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Declaring @Container as instance field (not static). Testcontainers creates a new container for EVERY test method — 50 tests = 50 container startups = very slow test suite.
common_gaps.remediation: Always declare @Container as static. Spring's @SpringBootTest reuses the application context across tests in the same class — the container should match this lifecycle. Rule: if the container is expensive to start (PostgreSQL, Kafka): static. If it must be fresh per test: use @BeforeEach to truncate tables instead of new container.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Test class has @Container without static keyword; test suite takes 5+ minutes even for a few tests

key_takeaways: Testcontainers: real Docker containers (not mocks) in integration tests
@Container static: one container per class (not per method)
JSONB, constraints, sequences: only caught with real PostgreSQL
Redis container: test expiry, data types (Sets, Lists)
Container reuse (.withReuse(true)): keep containers across test runs
CI: Docker daemon required (standard in GitHub Actions, GitLab CI)

self_check_questions.question: When should you use H2 vs Testcontainers for testing JPA repositories?
self_check_questions.answer: H2 is fine for: pure unit logic tests (business rules, calculations), testing JPA query syntax (JPQL), fast feedback during development (no Docker needed). Use Testcontainers (real PostgreSQL) for: JSONB columns (H2 doesn't enforce JSONB type), database-specific SQL (ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, RETURNING clause, pg_trgm), CHECK constraints (H2 ignores some), testing actual index behavior, any integration test that will run in CI before deploying to PostgreSQL. Rule of thumb: @DataJpaTest with H2 for unit-speed tests on pure JPQL; @SpringBootTest with PostgreSQL container for integration tests that must match production behavior.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Contract Testing with Pact — verify that consumer and provider agree on the API contract without running both services simultaneously.
